behaviors.description: The request sent to the external API always includes the complete list of valid taxonomy labels.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: failure is at the handoff boundary between request construction and API client dispatch, not just isolated label-format logic. REAL-INFRA: no real OS/network/subprocess is required to catch this; an in-process fake client that records outbound payload is sufficient. LLM-DEP: correctness does not depend on model judgment/content, only payload fields. MULTI-STEP: no multi-agentic chain. REJECTION-GATE: System/Agentic were considered and rejected because behavior text is about request contents ("includes the complete list of valid taxonomy labels"), not live transport behavior or model output quality.
behaviors.test_description: Wire classifier module to a fake API transport, invoke classify, and assert captured request contains every taxonomy label exactly as configured.

behaviors.description: When the API returns a label that is not in the taxonomy, the module returns "unknown" instead of forwarding the unexpected value to the caller.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: failure can occur at response parsing/validation boundary (API client output into module normalization/guard). REAL-INFRA: no real network needed; stubbed API response reproduces invalid-label path. LLM-DEP: not testing model quality, only guardrail behavior for a concrete invalid value. MULTI-STEP: none. REJECTION-GATE: Agentic rejected because behavior text explicitly fixes the input condition ("API returns a label that is not in the taxonomy"), so a deterministic stub can trigger the exact failure mode.
behaviors.test_description: Use a fake API returning an out-of-taxonomy label; assert classifier returns "unknown" and never exposes raw invalid label.

behaviors.description: For identical raw API responses, the module's normalization step always produces the same final label.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: this is deterministic normalization logic over identical inputs; no module handoff is required to expose failure. REAL-INFRA: none. LLM-DEP: none, because raw response is fixed and identical. MULTI-STEP: none. REJECTION-GATE: Integration rejected because behavior text scopes to "normalization step" and "identical raw API responses," which is a single local function/object boundary.
behaviors.test_description: Call normalization function repeatedly with the same raw response fixture and assert identical normalized label every run.

behaviors.description: On repeated live API calls for the same document and taxonomy, the returned label remains consistent unless the provider response itself changes.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: mocked responses would mask true provider/model variability. REAL-INFRA: live API calls are required, but the core risk is model/provider behavioral variability. LLM-DEP: yes; correctness depends on real model outputs across runs. MULTI-STEP: repeated calls are iterations of one agentic step, not a multi-stage pipeline. REJECTION-GATE: System rejected as minimum because behavior text targets label consistency under "repeated live API calls" where model output variability is central, which is higher than pure transport/HTTP behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Run N repeated real classifications on identical input with fixed settings; track provider raw responses and assert label stays stable whenever raw provider decision is unchanged.

behaviors.description: Request construction enforces the configured token budget before dispatch (for example truncate/reject according to policy).
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: failure can appear when budgeting logic interacts with dispatch path (e.g., reject should block send; truncate should alter sent payload). REAL-INFRA: no real OS/network needed; in-process fake transport can verify whether dispatch happened and what payload was sent. LLM-DEP: none; this is pre-dispatch policy enforcement. MULTI-STEP: none. REJECTION-GATE: System/Agentic rejected because behavior text says "before dispatch" and focuses on local policy ("truncate/reject"), not provider decision quality.
behaviors.test_description: Configure tight token budget, invoke classifier with oversized input, and assert policy outcome (truncate or reject) plus dispatch side effects via fake transport.

behaviors.description: Provider-reported token usage for a typical document stays within the configured per-call budget.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: a stubbed usage report can trivially pass and hide real provider token-accounting differences. REAL-INFRA: requires real API call and provider usage metadata. LLM-DEP: execution depends on a real AI API response path; per guidance, this defaults to Agentic even with deterministic assertion when validating behavior tied to real model service output fields. MULTI-STEP: no multi-stage agent chain. REJECTION-GATE: Integration rejected because behavior text explicitly requires "provider-reported token usage," which a local fake cannot faithfully validate against real provider accounting.
behaviors.test_description: Execute real classification calls for representative documents and assert provider-reported total/prompt/completion tokens do not exceed configured per-call budget.

behaviors.description: When the API signals that the request rate is too high, the module waits and retries rather than returning an error to the caller.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: key failure is retry orchestration across client, backoff policy, and caller contract; this is cross-module wiring. REAL-INFRA: a deterministic fake API returning rate-limit responses can reproduce and validate wait/retry behavior; real network is not strictly required for minimum detection. LLM-DEP: none; behavior concerns control flow on rate-limit signal, not model output quality. MULTI-STEP: not agentic sequencing. REJECTION-GATE: System rejected because behavior text is about handling a "rate too high" signal and retry policy, which is reproducible with in-process stubs and controllable clocks.
behaviors.test_description: Use fake API to return rate-limit error then success, with controllable clock/backoff; assert module delays and retries, then returns successful label instead of immediate error.
